title: AZ-104 — Microsoft Azure Administrator Associate
description: 50 hard questions covering Azure identities, storage, compute, networking, and monitoring. Updated 2026.

domains.name: Monitor and Back Up Azure Resources

questions.question: You manage an Azure subscription that contains 500 virtual machines across 50 resource groups. A new security policy mandates that all VMs must have a specific tag identifying the cost center. You need to apply this tag to all existing VMs without affecting running workloads. Which PowerShell command achieves this with the LEAST administrative effort?

choices.text: Get-AzResource -ResourceType 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines' | ForEach-Object { Update-AzTag -ResourceId $_.ResourceId -Tag @{'CostCenter'='IT-001'} -Operation Merge }
choices.explanation: Update-AzTag with the Merge operation adds the tag to existing resources without affecting other tags. Using Get-AzResource with a resource type filter targets only VMs across all resource groups in a single pipeline, minimizing administrative effort. This is a non-intrusive, in-place tag operation that does not restart VMs.

choices.text: Remove-AzTag -Name CostCenter; New-AzTag -Name CostCenter -Value IT-001; Apply-AzTag -ResourceId (Get-AzResource -ResourceType 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines').ResourceId
choices.explanation: Remove-AzTag removes the tag entirely before re-adding it — this would remove any existing CostCenter values from other resources. Apply-AzTag without the Merge operation overwrites all existing tags on the target resources.

choices.text: Get-AzVM | Set-AzResource -Tag @{'CostCenter'='IT-001'} -ApiVersion 2023-03-01
choices.explanation: Set-AzResource on a VM object uses the VM's resource group scope but does not support direct pipeline input from Get-AzVM. This approach would require explicit resource group looping and would not efficiently target all 500 VMs across 50 resource groups.

choices.text: az resource list --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines --query '[].id' -o tsv | xargs -I {} az tag update --tag CostCenter=IT-001 --operation Merge --ids {}
choices.explanation: az tag update with --operation Merge is the CLI equivalent of Update-AzTag — it adds the tag without removing existing tags. This command efficiently targets all VMs across subscriptions using a single pipeline.

questions.explanation: Update-AzTag with -Operation Merge and a pipeline from Get-AzResource is the most efficient, non-disruptive way to tag 500 VMs across 50 resource groups. Merge preserves existing tags, preventing accidental data loss.

questions.question: Your company uses Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) and Azure RBAC. You need to grant a contractor named Contractor1 access to manage only the virtual machines in ResourceGroup1, and nothing else. The access must expire automatically after 30 days. Which two steps achieve this?

choices.text: Create a Microsoft Entra ID user account for Contractor1 and configure the account with a set expiration date of 30 days from today.
choices.explanation: Entra ID user accounts can have a set expiration date, after which the account is automatically disabled. This is the correct way to enforce automatic 30-day access expiry at the identity level.

choices.text: Assign the 'Virtual Machine Contributor' RBAC role to Contractor1 scoped at the ResourceGroup1 level only.
choices.explanation: Virtual Machine Contributor at the resource group scope grants permission to manage VMs within that group and nothing else — it does not grant access to other resources, the VNet, storage, or subscription-level resources.

choices.text: Add Contractor1 to the 'Readers' group at the subscription level to ensure they can at least view all Azure resources before accessing VMs.
choices.explanation: Adding the contractor to Readers at subscription level grants read access to ALL resources in the subscription, which violates least privilege. The VM Contributor role alone is sufficient for the stated requirement.

choices.text: Create an Azure Policy assignment at the subscription level that enforces VM tagging and configure a deny effect to block creation of untagged VMs.
choices.explanation: Azure Policy controls resource creation compliance, not user access. This does not address the requirement to grant specific user access to specific VMs.

questions.explanation: Virtual Machine Contributor at resource group scope + Entra ID account expiration date is the complete least-privilege solution with automatic 30-day expiry.

questions.question: You are implementing Azure Policy to enforce that all storage accounts must use HTTPS traffic only and must be accessible only from specific trusted Microsoft Internet IP ranges. When you assign the policy, you discover that existing storage accounts are non-compliant. What is the correct sequence of steps to remediate the non-compliant storage accounts without disrupting existing applications?

choices.text: Run a policy remediation task to automatically update the 'enableHttpsTrafficOnly' and 'trustedAccess' properties on each non-compliant storage account. Verify the applications still function, then set the policy to 'Deny' for new resources to prevent future non-compliance.
choices.explanation: Remediation tasks automatically fix existing resources to match policy definitions. Setting the policy to 'Audit' first allows you to assess impact before switching to 'Deny' — this sequence ensures existing apps are not disrupted. 'Deny' should only be applied after verifying remediation doesn't break workloads.

choices.text: Immediately set the Azure Policy to 'Deny' effect to block all non-compliant storage accounts, forcing immediate compliance. Investigate application impact in parallel.
choices.explanation: Setting a Deny effect immediately blocks non-compliant resources — which would break existing applications using those storage accounts. The correct approach is remediation while still in Audit/Deny-NotSync, not immediate Deny enforcement on existing resources.

choices.text: Delete the non-compliant storage accounts and recreate them with the correct settings using an ARM template deployment that enforces the required properties.
choices.explanation: Deleting and recreating storage accounts is extremely disruptive — it destroys all stored data and breaks all application references. Policy remediation can update storage account properties in-place without data loss.

choices.text: Export the list of non-compliant storage accounts, create a support ticket for each account owner to manually update their storage accounts within 24 hours, and set the policy to 'Disabled' to pause enforcement during the remediation window.
choices.explanation: Manual remediation by account owners is slow, inconsistent, and error-prone. Azure Policy's remediation task automates this at scale. Disabling the policy entirely removes enforcement during the critical remediation window.

questions.explanation: Policy remediation tasks update resources in-place with a controlled sequence (Audit → remediate → verify → Deny), ensuring existing applications are not disrupted by automatic compliance fixes.

questions.question: Your organization has a Microsoft Entra ID tenant with 2,000 users. You need to grant members of the 'Project-Delta' group access to three Azure Storage accounts, but ONLY during business hours (Monday-Friday, 9 AM to 6 PM UTC). Outside of business hours, all access must be denied. How should you implement this?

choices.text: Create a Conditional Access policy targeting the Project-Delta group, with a session control that enforces a custom frequency of access (sign-in frequency = 1 hour) during the configured times (Monday-Friday, 9 AM-6 PM UTC). Enable the policy with a grant control requiring compliant device.
choices.explanation: Conditional Access sign-in frequency controls how often a user must re-authenticate, not whether they can access resources at all during specific hours. This does not achieve time-based access restriction — it only controls re-authentication frequency.

choices.text: Use Azure AD Privileged Identity Management (PIM) to assign eligible membership of the 'Storage Account Contributor' role to the Project-Delta group with an active assignment rule that activates only during Monday-Friday 9AM-6PM UTC time window.
choices.explanation: Azure AD PIM supports time-based role activation rules using the 'Assignment rule' field with an time-based filter on the group membership. However, the correct approach is to use PIM's 'Assignment schedule' feature with recurring time windows — this restricts when the role assignment is active.

choices.text: Configure Azure Storage Account firewall IP rules to whitelist only the corporate VPN IP range during business hours, and remove all IP rules outside business hours using an Azure Automation runbook triggered by a Logic App on a schedule.
choices.explanation: IP-based restriction at the storage account level is a valid approach, but this is not achievable through Conditional Access policies alone — it requires an external automation layer. The question asks about Entra ID group access control, not IP-based firewall rules.

choices.text: Use Azure AD Conditional Access policy with a 'Users and Groups' include for Project-Delta, and a 'Conditions' block that includes 'Client apps' set to 'Mobile apps and desktop clients' with 'Grant' access only during named locations configured for the corporate office IP range.
choices.explanation: Named locations in Conditional Access restrict access by IP range, not by time window. This would restrict access to corporate office IPs, not enforce a time-of-day restriction on group access.

questions.explanation: Azure AD PIM with time-based assignment schedules is the Entra ID-native mechanism for restricting role activation to specific recurring time windows — it is the correct solution for time-based group access control.

questions.question: Your company recently acquired another business and you need to grant 200 new employees access to Azure resources. The new employees will be managed in Microsoft Entra ID from the acquired company's tenant. You need to ensure these employees can access specific Azure resources using their existing corporate email credentials. What should you do?

choices.text: Create Microsoft Entra B2B guest user invitations for each of the 200 employees, pointing to their corporate email addresses from the acquired company's Entra tenant. Assign RBAC roles to these guest users scoped to the required resources.
choices.explanation: Entra B2B guest invitations allow users from an external Entra tenant (the acquired company's) to authenticate with their own corporate credentials and be granted access to your tenant's resources via RBAC. This is the standard approach for granting access to users from another Entra organization.

choices.text: Create regular Entra member users for the 200 employees in your tenant and configure their UserPrincipalName to match their corporate email from the acquired company's domain.
choices.explanation: You cannot create member users with an external company's domain in your Entra tenant — UPNs must belong to a verified domain in the local tenant. Users from another company cannot have member accounts in your tenant without first being invited as B2B guests.

choices.text: Configure Azure AD Connect to synchronize the acquired company's on-premises Active Directory with your Entra tenant, creating linked accounts for all 200 employees.
choices.explanation: Azure AD Connect synchronizes from a SINGLE on-premises AD to a SINGLE Entra tenant. You cannot use AD Connect to pull users from another company's on-premises AD into your Entra tenant.

choices.text: Use Azure AD B2C to create local accounts for the 200 employees in your tenant's B2C directory, allowing them to sign in with their corporate credentials via a custom identity provider.
choices.explanation: Azure AD B2C is for consumer-facing identity scenarios, not enterprise employee identity scenarios. B2C is not designed for granting employees from another company access to corporate Azure resources.

questions.explanation: Entra B2B guest invitations are the correct, supported mechanism for granting users from an external Entra organization access to your Azure resources using their existing corporate credentials.

questions.question: You need to audit all role assignments in your Azure subscription to identify who has 'Owner' role at the subscription scope. You must produce a report listing each user's display name, role assignment name, and whether the assignment was inherited from a management group. Which command provides this information most efficiently?

choices.text: Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope '/subscriptions/{subId}' -RoleDefinitionName 'Owner' | Select-Object DisplayName, RoleAssignmentId, @{N='Inherited';E={$_.Scope -eq '/'}} | Format-Table
choices.explanation: Get-AzRoleAssignment with the -Scope parameter returns all role assignments at that scope. The Scope property reveals whether the assignment was created at that scope or inherited from above (inherited assignments show the inherited scope). This provides the full picture in a single command.

choices.text: Get-AzADUser | ForEach-Object { Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $_.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Owner' }
choices.explanation: This iterates over all AD users (thousands) and checks their role assignments — extremely slow and unnecessary. Get-AzRoleAssignment directly filters by role name and scope, returning only the relevant assignments.

choices.text: az role assignment list --role 'Owner' --scope /subscriptions/{subId} --include-inherited
choices.explanation: az role assignment list with --include-inherited returns both directly assigned and inherited role assignments, which is the correct way to capture management-group-inherited assignments. This is more efficient than the PowerShell approach for this specific query.

choices.text: Use Azure Policy's 'Audit role assignments' initiative to generate a compliance report showing all Owner role assignments at the subscription scope.
choices.explanation: Azure Policy audit initiatives report on policy compliance, not RBAC role assignments. They are not the right tool for auditing who has specific roles on a subscription.

questions.explanation: Get-AzRoleAssignment with scope filter or az role assignment list --include-inherited are both efficient single-command approaches — the key is including inherited assignments to capture management-group-level grants.

questions.question: You have an Azure Storage account with hierarchical namespace disabled (not an ADLS Gen2 account). You need to provide time-limited read-only access to a specific blob container for an external partner without sharing the storage account key. The access must expire after 30 days. Which solution provides this access?

choices.text: Generate a Shared Access Signature (SAS) token at the container scope with read (r) permission, valid for 30 days. Provide the external partner with the SAS token and blob container URL. Require HTTPS when using the SAS.
choices.explanation: A container-scoped SAS token with read-only permissions provides exactly the required access: time-limited (30 days), scope-restricted (only that container), no account key required, and HTTPS enforcement prevents token interception. This is the standard solution for external partner blob access.

choices.text: Create an Access Policy on the container with a 30-day expiry and issue a storage account key-based credential to the external partner.
choices.explanation: Access policies on containers apply to Shared Access Signatures only, not to storage account key-based access. Sharing the storage account key gives the partner full access to ALL containers and services in the account — far beyond the required scope.

choices.text: Enable Azure AD authentication on the storage account and assign the external partner's Entra ID guest user the 'Storage Blob Data Reader' role at the container scope.
choices.explanation: Azure AD authentication for blob access requires the user to be a member or B2B guest in YOUR Entra tenant. An external partner from another company cannot authenticate with their own Entra credentials against your storage account unless you configure cross-tenant B2B collaboration, which is complex.

choices.text: Use Azure Private Link to expose the storage account on a private endpoint and provide the partner with VPN credentials to access the private endpoint.
choices.explanation: Private Link provides network isolation, not access authorization. Without additional access controls, the partner still cannot read blob data. Private Link also doesn't provide time-limited access — VPN credentials are typically long-lived.

questions.explanation: Container-scoped SAS token with 30-day expiration and HTTPS enforcement is the correct, minimal-scope, time-limited solution for external blob access on standard (non-ADLS) storage accounts.

questions.question: You have an Azure Storage account used for storing virtual machine backup VHDs. The storage account is currently configured with ZRS (Zone-Redundant Storage) and you need to change to GZRS (Geo-Zone-Redundant Storage) to protect against regional outages while maintaining zone redundancy within the primary region. What is the correct procedure for this migration?

choices.text: In the Azure portal, navigate to the storage account's 'Configuration' blade and change the 'Redundancy' setting from ZRS to GZRS. Azure will perform an asynchronous migration of all existing data to the new redundancy configuration without downtime.
choices.explanation: Azure Storage supports online redundancy migration — you can change from ZRS to GZRS (or any other redundancy type except from LRS to GRS/GZRS without going through ZRS first) via the portal or CLI without downtime. Azure manages the data copy and redundancy re-configuration transparently.

choices.text: Create a new storage account with GZRS redundancy. Use AzCopy to copy all VHDs from the existing ZRS account to the new account. Update all VM configurations to point to the new account. Delete the old account after validation.
choices.explanation: AzCopy migration is required only when online migration is not supported (e.g., LRS to GRS). ZRS to GZRS is a supported online migration path — manual migration adds unnecessary downtime, cost, and complexity.

choices.text: Enable the 'Allow migration' feature flag in the storage account's ARM resource definition, then perform a PATCH operation on the storage account resource to update the sku.name property to 'GZRS'.
choices.explanation: Feature flags are not required for standard redundancy migration — the sku update is supported as a direct PUT/PATCH operation on the storage account resource without requiring a feature flag.

choices.text: ZRS cannot be directly migrated to GZRS. You must first migrate from ZRS to LRS, then from LRS to GZRS, as these are the only supported single-step migration paths.
choices.explanation: This was true in older versions of Azure Storage but is no longer accurate. ZRS to GZRS is now a supported direct migration path in the Azure portal and CLI.

questions.explanation: Azure Storage supports direct online migration from ZRS to GZRS without downtime or manual data copy — the portal or CLI handles the asynchronous data replication transparently.

questions.question: You have an Azure storage account named 'storageprod001' used by 30 Azure VMs for boot diagnostics. You need to ensure that boot diagnostics logs are retained for 30 days and then automatically deleted to reduce storage costs. The storage account uses the 'Standard_LRS' SKU. How should you configure lifecycle management for this requirement?

choices.text: Create a lifecycle management policy with a rule targeting the 'bootdiagnostics-' prefix in the storage account. Set the rule to delete blobs with a lastModified date greater than 30 days on a daily schedule.
choices.explanation: Azure Storage lifecycle management policies can target blobs by name prefix (e.g., 'bootdiagnostics-') and delete them after a specified number of days since last modification. This is the native, zero-cost way to auto-purge old boot diagnostics without manual intervention.

choices.text: Enable soft delete on the storage account with a 30-day retention period. This will automatically delete boot diagnostics older than 30 days during the next blob garbage collection cycle.
choices.explanation: Soft delete protects against accidental deletion by retaining deleted blobs for the recovery period — it does NOT automatically delete old blobs. Soft delete retains deleted data, not purge old data.

choices.text: Configure Azure Backup Vault with a 30-day retention policy for the storage account, which will automatically remove backup snapshots older than 30 days.
choices.explanation: Azure Backup is for backing up VMs and workloads, not for managing boot diagnostics lifecycle. Boot diagnostics are stored as page blobs in the storage account, not as backup items in a Backup Vault.

choices.text: Create an Azure Automation runbook that runs daily, queries all boot diagnostics blobs older than 30 days using Get-AzStorageBlob, and calls Remove-AzStorageBlob to delete them.
choices.explanation: An Automation runbook is an unnecessary and costly solution when lifecycle management policies natively handle this. Custom runbooks introduce maintenance burden, credential management for Automation accounts, and potential failure points that the native policy avoids.

questions.explanation: Lifecycle management policies with a prefix-based rule targeting 'bootdiagnostics-' and a 30-day delete rule is the native, cost-free, automation-native way to auto-purge old boot diagnostics.

questions.question: You have an Azure Storage account behind a private endpoint. You need to allow an on-premises application server to write backup files to the storage account using the SMB protocol. The on-premises server cannot use Azure AD authentication. Which storage feature must be enabled and how must the connection be established?

choices.text: Enable Azure Files SMB authentication with 'Require minimum SMB version 3.0' and use an Azure File Sync with a storage sync service endpoint on the private VNet to bridge the on-premises SMB traffic through the private endpoint.
choices.explanation: Azure File Sync is for caching Azure Files shares on-premises for faster access, not for writing backups FROM on-premises TO Azure. It doesn't solve the SMB-over-private-endpoint problem for on-premises write traffic.

choices.text: Configure the storage account to allow trusted Azure services and add the on-premises server's public IP to the storage account's exception list in the firewall settings.
choices.explanation: Storage account firewall IP exceptions allow access from specific public IPs but do not enable SMB protocol access over a private endpoint. The private endpoint routes traffic through the VNet, not the public internet.

choices.text: Enable the 'Allow SMB' property on the storage account and configure a Site-to-Site VPN or ExpressRoute between the on-premises network and the Azure VNet containing the private endpoint. The on-premises server then mounts the Azure Files share using the private endpoint's IP address over the encrypted VPN tunnel.
choices.explanation: Azure Files SMB access requires either AD authentication (not available on-premises) or the storage account's 'Allow SMB' flag for compatible on-premises scenarios. A VPN or ExpressRoute connects the on-premises server to the VNet so SMB traffic reaches the private endpoint. This is the supported hybrid access pattern for on-premises SMB access to private-endpoint-protected storage accounts.

choices.text: Use AzCopy over HTTPS to transfer files from the on-premises server to the storage account's blob service behind the private endpoint, bypassing the need for SMB entirely.
choices.explanation: AzCopy over HTTPS requires the storage account's blob service to be reachable from the internet (or a VPN). With a private endpoint, AzCopy from on-premises without a VPN cannot reach the storage account. This doesn't solve the SMB requirement for backup files.

questions.explanation: Azure Files with SMB and a VPN/ExpressRoute connection is the supported pattern for on-premises SMB write access to a private-endpoint-protected storage account when Azure AD authentication is not available.

questions.question: You have an Azure Storage account used to store files that are accessed frequently for the first 30 days after upload, rarely for the next 60 days, and then never accessed again. You need to implement a cost-optimized storage tier strategy that automatically moves files through the appropriate tiers. Which lifecycle management rule configuration achieves this with the lowest cost?

choices.text: Configure a lifecycle management policy with two rules: Rule 1 — move blobs to Hot tier on creation (immediate); Rule 2 — move blobs from Hot to Cool tier after 30 days of last access; Rule 3 — delete blobs after 90 days of last modification.
choices.explanation: Azure Storage lifecycle management supports 'lastAccessTime' based tiering, which moves blobs based on the last time they were READ (not modified). This is more cost-efficient than modification-based rules because it correctly handles read-heavy workloads. The rule sequence (Hot → Cool → Delete) at 30/90 days is the standard recommended pattern for this access pattern.

choices.text: Configure the storage account's default access tier to 'Cool' and use a lifecycle rule to move blobs to 'Hot' after 30 days and delete after 90 days.
choices.explanation: Setting the default tier to Cool means newly uploaded files are stored in Cool (cheaper) but with higher read access costs — the opposite of the requirement which requires files to be in Hot for the first 30 days (frequently accessed). The default tier should be Hot, not Cool.

choices.text: Use Azure Blob Storage's 'Auto-Tier' feature which automatically places blobs in the optimal tier based on access patterns without requiring any lifecycle management rules.
choices.explanation: Azure Blob Storage does not have an 'Auto-Tier' feature. Tier optimization requires explicit lifecycle management rules based on last access time or last modification time.

choices.text: Create a lifecycle management rule with 'lastModified' time condition to move blobs to Cool after 30 days and delete after 90 days. Do NOT set a Hot tier rule since blobs default to Hot on creation.
choices.explanation: Blobs in Azure Storage default to Hot tier when created. A single rule: move to Cool at 30 days, delete at 90 days, achieves the required lifecycle without needing an explicit 'move to Hot' rule on creation. This is the most efficient single-rule configuration.

questions.explanation: Hot (default on creation) → Cool at 30 days (lastAccessTime) → Delete at 90 days (lastModified) is the minimum-rule, maximum-savings lifecycle policy for this access pattern.

questions.question: You have an Azure VM named 'WebServer01' that you need to move from the East US region to the West Europe region. The VM has an unmanaged OS disk stored in a storage account in East US. You must complete the move with the least administrative effort and minimal downtime. Which sequence of steps achieves this?

choices.text: Stop the VM. Create a snapshot of the OS disk. Copy the snapshot to a storage account in West Europe. Create a new managed disk in West Europe from the snapshot. Recreate the VM in West Europe using the new managed disk. Update DNS and traffic routing.
choices.explanation: This approach creates an unmanaged disk first, then migrates manually. Since the source is unmanaged, using Azure Site Recovery (ASR) for the migration would be simpler and more supported than manual snapshot copy and VM recreation.

choices.text: Use Azure Site Recovery to replicate WebServer01 from East US to West Europe. Perform a planned failover to West Europe once replication is complete. The replication process handles disk conversion from unmanaged to managed automatically.
choices.explanation: Azure Site Recovery (ASR) supports VM migration between regions and automatically handles disk conversion (unmanaged to managed) during replication. ASR is the Microsoft-recommended, minimally-disruptive tool for cross-region VM migration. A planned failover ensures minimal downtime.

choices.text: Delete the VM (keeping the unmanaged disk), use AzCopy to copy the unmanaged OS disk VHD file from the East US storage account to a West Europe storage account, then recreate the VM in West Europe pointing to the copied VHD.
choices.explanation: This approach is manually intensive and requires deleting the VM before confirming the copy succeeded. If the AzCopy fails or is incomplete, the VM cannot be recovered without redeploying from scratch. ASR handles this migration atomically with built-in validation.

choices.text: Use the Azure Resource Mover to move WebServer01 from East US to West Europe in a single operation, which handles the storage account migration and disk conversion automatically.
choices.explanation: Azure Resource Mover is designed specifically for cross-region resource migration. It orchestrates the entire move including disk conversion and network resource updates in a single tracked operation, minimizing administrative effort compared to manual snapshot-and-copy approaches.

questions.explanation: Azure Site Recovery or Resource Mover are the recommended tools for cross-region VM migration — both handle unmanaged-to-managed disk conversion and network reconfiguration automatically, minimizing downtime and administrative effort.

questions.question: You have a Virtual Machine Scale Set (VMSS) with 5 VMs running a web application in a load-balanced configuration. You need to deploy a new version of the application with zero downtime and the ability to roll back quickly if the deployment fails. Which VMSS upgrade policy should you configure?

choices.text: Set the upgradePolicy mode to 'Automatic'. Configure an Application Health Extension on the VMSS to report the health of the application. Azure will automatically update all VM instances in the VMSS simultaneously and roll back automatically if health checks fail.
choices.explanation: 'Automatic' mode updates ALL instances simultaneously — this causes downtime because all old instances are replaced at once. Automatic mode also does NOT perform automatic rollback on failure — it continues through failures. This is not zero-downtime deployment.

choices.text: Set the upgradePolicy mode to 'Rolling' with a batch size of 1 VM and a pause duration of 0 minutes between batches. Enable upgrade pre-check with an Application Health Extension. This will update one VM at a time, keeping the other 4 serving traffic.
choices.explanation: Rolling upgrade policy with batch size 1 ensures only 1 VM is updated at a time, maintaining 4/5 capacity throughout the upgrade. Application Health Extension provides application-level health reporting so Azure only proceeds with the next batch when the updated VM's application is confirmed healthy. Zero minutes pause between batches maximizes speed while still being sequential.

choices.text: Set the upgradePolicy mode to 'Manual'. Manually upgrade each VM instance one by one using the Update-AzVmssInstance command in a loop, monitoring application health between each update.
choices.explanation: Manual mode requires manual intervention for each instance update — it doesn't provide automatic health-based progression. If a bad update is applied to all instances, manual mode offers no automated rollback capability. Azure Resource Manager templates with desired state configuration are a better approach for manual-but-structured rolling deployments.

choices.text: Use the 'Automatic' upgrade policy with a health probe attached to the load balancer so Azure automatically detects failed instances and replaces them with new ones from the updated image.
choices.explanation: A load balancer health probe detects unhealthy VMs and removes them from rotation, but it does not prevent the upgrade from continuing to update all instances simultaneously. Azure still replaces all instances at once in Automatic mode, causing a brief period where no healthy instances exist during the cutover.

questions.explanation: Rolling upgrade with batch size 1 + Application Health Extension provides zero-downtime deployment by maintaining N-1 capacity throughout the upgrade and automatically pausing on health failures.

questions.question: You are configuring availability options for a two-tier application where the web tier consists of Azure VMs behind an Application Gateway and the database tier consists of VMs with SQL Server. The SLA requirement is 99.99% uptime for the web tier and 99.9% for the database tier. Which availability configuration is correct for each tier?

choices.text: Web tier: deploy VMs in an Availability Set with 3 fault domains and 5 update domains. Database tier: deploy VMs in a separate Availability Zone spanning 3 Azure zones.
choices.explanation: Availability Sets provide 99.95% SLA, not 99.99%. For 99.99% SLA, you need Availability Zones (which require VMSS with zones or multiple VMs in different zones). Also, mixing Availability Set and Availability Zone in the same answer is inconsistent — each tier should use one or the other based on its SLA requirement.

choices.text: Web tier: deploy VMs in a VMSS with 'Standard' SKU load balancer and 2 VMs in 2 different Availability Zones (zones 1 and 2). Database tier: deploy VMs in a separate Availability Set with 3 fault domains and 5 update domains.
choices.explanation: VMSS with 2 VMs in 2 zones provides 99.99% SLA but only if you use a zonal SKU load balancer. A Standard SKU load balancer with zone-redundant configuration is required. The database tier in an Availability Set provides only 99.95% SLA, not the required 99.9%.

choices.text: Web tier: deploy VMs in a VMSS with 2 or more VMs spread across 2 or more Availability Zones, using a Standard SKU load balancer with zone-redundant configuration. This achieves 99.99% SLA. Database tier: deploy VMs in an Availability Set with 2 or more VMs, which provides 99.95% SLA.
choices.explanation: Availability Sets with 2 VMs provide 99.95% SLA, not 99.9%. For 99.9% SLA on the database tier, you need at least 2 VMs in an Availability Set (which actually gives 99.95%). The answer must accurately reflect Azure's actual SLA tiers, not approximate them.

choices.text: Web tier: deploy 2 or more VMs in a VMSS spread across at least 2 Availability Zones with a Standard SKU load balancer with zone-redundant or zonal configuration. This achieves 99.99% SLA. Database tier: deploy 2 VMs in an Availability Set with managed disks, which provides 99.95% SLA.
choices.explanation: Azure VM SLA: 2+ VMs in 2+ AZs = 99.99%. 2+ VMs in an Availability Set = 99.95%. There is no 99.9% SLA tier for VMs — the tiers are 99.9% (single VM with premium managed disk), 99.95% (Availability Set), and 99.99% (Availability Zones). Since no 99.9% option exists for multi-VM database tier, Availability Set (99.95%) is the closest and highest available.

questions.explanation: Azure VM SLA tiers are: 99.9% (single VM + managed disks), 99.95% (Availability Set, 2+ VMs), 99.99% (Availability Zones, 2+ VMs in 2+ zones). For the stated SLAs, web tier needs Zones and database tier needs Availability Set.

questions.question: You need to create an Azure Virtual Machine running Windows Server 2022 with a 2TB OS disk and a 10TB data disk. You must use Azure managed disks. The solution must minimize cost for the OS disk (which will have moderate write activity) while ensuring the data disk can handle 50,000 IOPS for a database workload. Which managed disk configuration for the OS disk and data disk meets these requirements?

choices.text: OS disk: Standard SSD (E4 S2) with 128 GiB size. Data disk: Ultra Disk (U500) with 1024 GiB size, configured for 50,000 IOPS and 384 MB/s throughput.
choices.explanation: Standard SSD E4 S2 provides sufficient performance for a moderate-write OS disk at minimum cost. Ultra Disk U500 provides 50,000 IOPS and 384 MB/s throughput at the 1,024 GiB size — exactly matching the database IOPS requirement. Ultra Disk is the only managed disk type that guarantees specific IOPS and throughput values.

choices.text: OS disk: Standard HDD (S4) with 128 GiB. Data disk: Premium SSD P50 with 4,096 GiB, which provides 50,000 IOPS and 250 MB/s throughput.
choices.explanation: Standard HDD is not appropriate for a Windows Server OS disk even for moderate write activity — Standard SSD E4 is the minimum recommended tier for OS disks. Premium SSD P50 provides 7,500 IOPS (not 50,000 IOPS) and 250 MB/s throughput — it cannot meet the 50,000 IOPS requirement.

choices.text: OS disk: Standard SSD E10 with 128 GiB. Data disk: Premium SSD P80 with 8,192 GiB and 80,000 IOPS, configured for 50,000 IOPS using聚束.
choices.explanation: Premium SSD P80 is 32,767 GiB (32 TiB) max, not 8,192 GiB, and it doesn't exist as a standard size. Premium SSDs max at P70 (32,767 GiB). No single Premium SSD disk can provide 50,000 IOPS — the maximum for a single Premium SSD disk is 7,500 IOPS (P50). You would need multiple disks in a storage spaces configuration to achieve 50,000 IOPS.

choices.text: OS disk: Premium SSD P1 with 4 GiB. Data disk: Ultra Disk U1000 with 65,536 GiB size, providing 50,000 IOPS.
choices.explanation: P1 is only 4 GiB and cannot hold a 2TB OS disk. Azure managed OS disks come in predefined sizes: Standard (S4=64 GiB, S6=128 GiB, S10=128 GiB, S15=256 GiB, S20=512 GiB, S30=1024 GiB) and Premium (E1=4 GiB, E2=8 GiB, E4=32 GiB, E6=64 GiB, E10=128 GiB, E15=256 GiB, E20=512 GiB, E30=1024 GiB, E40=2048 GiB). An E40 at 2048 GiB provides 2TB OS disk capacity at minimum cost for moderate writes.

questions.explanation: Standard SSD E4 (OS) for cost efficiency + Ultra Disk U500 (data) for guaranteed 50K IOPS. Standard SSD E4 S2 is the minimum-cost OS disk tier with managed disks. Premium SSD maxes at 7,500 IOPS per disk — Ultra Disk is required for 50,000 IOPS workloads.

questions.question: You have an Azure VM that runs a critical line-of-business application. You need to ensure that if the Azure host server experiences a hardware failure, the VM automatically restarts on a new host within the same Azure region with minimal data loss. The application writes data to a data disk attached to the VM. Which SLA guarantee does this scenario describe and which feature provides it?

choices.text: This describes the 99.95% SLA for VMs with Premium SSD managed disks in an Availability Set. Azure's host failure recovery automatically migrates the VM to a new physical host without data loss on Premium SSD managed disks.
choices.explanation: Azure's 99.95% SLA (for 2+ VMs in an Availability Set or for a single VM with Premium SSD) includes host failure recovery — when Azure detects a hardware failure, it automatically migrates the VM to a new host in the same region. Premium SSD managed disks are not affected by host failures (they are separated from the host hardware), so no data loss occurs on attached managed disks.

choices.text: This describes the 99.9% SLA for single VMs with Standard SSD managed disks. The SLA guarantees automatic restart within 15 minutes of a host failure.
choices.explanation: 99.9% SLA applies to single VMs with Standard SSD. The SLA does not specify a 15-minute restart window — it specifies that Microsoft will endeavor to make the VM available. The actual SLA terms don't include a specific restart time commitment.

choices.text: This describes Azure's 99.99% SLA for VMs deployed across 2 or more Availability Zones. The VM automatically fails over to a new zone when the host server in the original zone fails.
choices.explanation: 99.99% SLA requires VMs in 2 or more Availability Zones. The question says 'within the same Azure region' which implies a single-zone or Availability Set deployment, not a zonal deployment. Zonal deployment spans zones, not 'within the same region' in the sense of a single location.

choices.text: This describes a guarantee that only Azure Reserved VM Instances provide — in exchange for a 1-year or 3-year commitment, Azure guarantees host maintenance within 30 minutes of a hardware failure.
choices.explanation: Reserved Instances provide a billing discount, not a different SLA guarantee. The host failure recovery SLA is the same for Reserved and pay-as-you-go VMs. The SLA terms are independent of the RI commitment.

questions.explanation: Host failure recovery is included in Azure's 99.95% SLA for VMs using Premium SSD managed disks (2+ VMs in AS or single VM with Premium SSD). The VM automatically migrates to a new host — managed disk data is preserved because disks are decoupled from host hardware.

questions.question: You need to configure autoscale for a VMSS that runs a stateless web application. The requirements are: scale out when CPU exceeds 70% for 5 minutes, scale in when CPU drops below 30% for 10 minutes, minimum 2 instances, maximum 10 instances. Which Azure autoscale rule configuration is correct?

choices.text: Scale-out rule: metric = 'Percentage CPU', operator = 'GreaterThan', threshold = 70, duration = 5 minutes, scale action = Increase count by 1. Scale-in rule: metric = 'Percentage CPU', operator = 'LessThan', threshold = 30, duration = 10 minutes, scale action = Decrease count by 1. Instance limits: minimum = 2, maximum = 10.
choices.explanation: This configuration correctly implements the specified thresholds and durations. Duration specifies how long the condition must be met before scaling triggers — this prevents flapping (rapid scale up/down). A 5-minute duration for scale-out and 10-minute for scale-in is a standard pattern that prevents oscillation. Scale by 1 instance at a time is the safest approach for a stateless web app.

choices.text: Scale-out rule: metric = 'Percentage CPU', operator = 'GreaterThan', threshold = 70, duration = 10 minutes, scale action = Increase count by 5. Scale-in rule: metric = 'Percentage CPU', operator = 'LessThan', threshold = 30, duration = 5 minutes, scale action = Decrease count by 2. Instance limits: minimum = 2, maximum = 10.
choices.explanation: Duration of 10 minutes for scale-out is too long — it delays the response to sustained high CPU. Duration of 5 minutes for scale-in is too short — it could cause premature scale-in during brief CPU dips. Increasing by 5 and decreasing by 2 instances at a time can cause oscillation and is too aggressive for a stateless web app.

choices.text: Scale-out rule: metric = 'Memory Utilized', operator = 'GreaterThan', threshold = 70, duration = 5 minutes, scale action = Increase count to 10. Scale-in rule: metric = 'Memory Utilized', operator = 'LessThan', threshold = 30, duration = 10 minutes, scale action = Decrease count to 2.
choices.explanation: The requirement specifies CPU, not memory. Scale-to-specific-count actions (Increase to 10, Decrease to 2) instead of scale-by-count actions (Increase by 1, Decrease by 1) can cause immediate over-scaling or under-scaling if the rules fire at unexpected times. Scale by count is the recommended pattern.

choices.text: Scale-out rule: metric = 'Percentage CPU', operator = 'GreaterThan', threshold = 70, duration = 0 minutes, scale action = Increase count by 1. Scale-in rule: metric = 'Percentage CPU', operator = 'LessThan', threshold = 30, duration = 0 minutes, scale action = Decrease count by 1. Instance limits: minimum = 2, maximum = 10.
choices.explanation: Duration = 0 minutes means the scale action fires immediately on a single metric reading, without confirming the condition is sustained. This causes flapping — a brief CPU spike triggers immediate scale-out, then the next reading drops back below threshold triggering scale-in. Duration should always be set to prevent flapping.

questions.explanation: Autoscale with duration settings prevents flapping: 5-minute duration for scale-out confirms sustained load before reacting, and 10-minute duration for scale-in prevents premature removal of instances during brief CPU dips.

questions.question: You are deploying a new Azure VM using an ARM template. The VM must be deployed with the latest Windows Server 2022 Azure Edition image from the Microsoft server catalog, with a managed identity enabled, and with the VM's OS disk encrypted using Azure Disk Encryption (ADE) with a key from the customer's Azure Key Vault. The Key Vault is in a different resource group. Which ARM template resource configuration is correct?

choices.text: In the VM resource, set 'identity.type' to 'SystemAssigned'. In a separate 'Microsoft.Compute/diskEncryptionSet' resource, reference the Key Vault's encryption key (keyUrl) and set 'type': 'EncryptionAtRestWithCustomerKey'. Reference the diskEncryptionSet in the VM's OS disk 'encryptionSettings' property.
choices.explanation: ADE with Key Vault (for OS disks) requires the Key Vault to be in the same subscription and the disk encryption set must reference the Key Vault key. However, for OS disk encryption with ADE on Azure VMs (not HCI or VMs with HBI), the encryptionSettings are applied via the VM's 'osDisk.encryptionSettings' property, not via a separate diskEncryptionSet resource.

choices.text: Use the 'imageReference' publisher 'MicrosoftWindowsServer', offer 'WindowsServer', sku '2022-datacenter-azure-edition'. Enable systemAssigned identity on the VM. Create a keyVaultDiskEncryptionSet resource linking the Key Vault to a diskEncryptionSet, and reference the diskEncryptionSet in the VM's managed disks osDisk section.
choices.explanation: Azure Disk Encryption (ADE) with customer-managed keys requires a diskEncryptionSet resource that references the Key Vault key. For the OS disk, the diskEncryptionSet is referenced in the managedDisk.encryptionSettings of the VM resource. The Key Vault must have 'Disk Encryption' resource provider access enabled.

choices.text: Use the imageReference with '2022-datacenter-l2hs' sku which is the Windows Server 2022 Azure Edition image. Enable the 'encryptionAtHost' property on the VM resource to encrypt the OS disk at the host level without requiring a separate Key Vault.
choices.explanation: '2022-datacenter-l2hs' is not a valid Windows Server 2022 Azure Edition sku. The correct Azure Edition sku is '2022-datacenter-azure-edition' or '2022-datacenter-azure-edition-smalldisk'. Also, encryptionAtHost is a host-level encryption separate from ADE — it encrypts the VM's temp disk and OS disk at the hypervisor level but does not use customer-managed keys from Key Vault.

choices.text: Deploy the VM with the correct image reference, enable systemAssigned identity, and add a vaultSecret resource in the VM extension profile to inject the Key Vault encryption key into the VM at boot time for ADE activation.
choices.explanation: VM extensions cannot be used to inject Key Vault encryption keys for ADE activation. ADE for OS disks requires the encryption to be configured via the VM's disk encryption settings referencing a diskEncryptionSet, not via extensions.

questions.explanation: ADE with customer-managed keys requires a diskEncryptionSet resource referencing the Key Vault key, with the diskEncryptionSet referenced in the VM's managed OS disk encryptionSettings. The Key Vault must have the 'Microsoft.Compute/diskEncryptionSets' resource provider registered.

questions.question: You have an Azure VM that is configured with the 'Shutdown' option set to 'Always' in the Azure portal (the 'Shutdown' option in the VM's Operations > Auto-shutdown blade). When the VM is shutdown from within the operating system (using WindowsShutdown or sudo shutdown now), what happens to the VM?

choices.text: The VM enters the 'Stopped (deallocated)' state. No compute charges are incurred. The public IP address (if dynamic) is released. The managed OS disk and data disks remain and charges continue for storage.
choices.explanation: OS-initiated shutdown on an Azure VM (not a restart) triggers Azure's standard shutdown behavior — the VM goes to 'Stopped (deallocated)' state. Compute charges stop (you pay only for storage). Dynamic public IPs are released. Auto-shutdown does NOT deallocate the VM — that requires explicit Stop-AzVm or portal 'Stop' action. But OS-initiated shutdown IS the Stop action in Azure — it does deallocate.

choices.text: The VM enters the 'Running' state as the Azure fabric detects the OS is shutting down and immediately restarts it to maintain the desired state configured by the Auto-shutdown policy.
choices.explanation: Azure's Auto-shutdown feature only sends a shutdown notification to the VM — it does not restart a VM that has been shut down from within the OS. The VM remains in the stopped state.

choices.text: The VM enters the 'Stopped (allocated)' state. Compute charges continue because the VM is still provisioned. The public IP address and disks remain attached.
choices.explanation: OS-initiated shutdown using the standard shutdown command (not the Azure portal 'Stop' button specifically) DOES deallocate the VM, putting it in 'Stopped (deallocated)' state. Only Stop-AzVm without -StayProvisioning causes deallocation. Actually, in Azure, 'shutdown from within OS' IS equivalent to a Stop action with deallocation by default.

choices.text: The VM remains in the 'Running' state but the guest agent stops all services. The Auto-shutdown policy then separately shuts down the VM at the scheduled time.
choices.explanation: Auto-shutdown and OS-initiated shutdown are independent actions. The OS shutdown takes immediate effect and puts the VM into a stopped/deallocated state. The Auto-shutdown schedule only fires at the configured time and has no effect if the VM is already stopped.

questions.explanation: Shutting down from within the OS using standard shutdown commands triggers Azure's deallocation process — the VM enters 'Stopped (deallocated)' state, compute charges stop, and dynamic public IPs are released. Storage charges continue.

questions.question: You are configuring Azure Bastion on a VNet to provide secure RDP and SSH access to VMs without exposing their public IPs. The VNet contains three subnets: Subnet-A (10.0.0.0/24), Subnet-B (10.0.1.0/24), and AzureBastionSubnet (10.0.2.0/24). You need to ensure Bastion can connect to VMs in both Subnet-A and Subnet-B. Which configuration is required?

choices.text: Deploy Azure Bastion in the AzureBastionSubnet. Configure the NSG on AzureBastionSubnet to allow inbound TCP ports 443 from the internet and outbound TCP 3389/22 to the target VM subnets (Subnet-A and Subnet-B). Assign the 'BastionHost' role to the Bastion resource on a resource group containing the target VMs.
choices.explanation: Azure Bastion requires its own dedicated subnet named 'AzureBastionSubnet' (/26 or larger). The Bastion NSG must allow inbound 443 from internet and outbound RDP (3389) and SSH (22) to the target VM subnets. The BastionHost role on the resource group allows Bastion to connect to VMs in that scope without needing individual VM-level permissions.

choices.text: Deploy Azure Bastion in Subnet-A and configure Subnet-B NSG to allow TCP 443 from Subnet-A. This allows Bastion in Subnet-A to proxy RDP to Subnet-B.
choices.explanation: Bastion MUST be deployed in its own dedicated subnet named 'AzureBastionSubnet' — it cannot share a general-purpose subnet like Subnet-A. This is a hard requirement.

choices.text: Deploy Azure Bastion in the AzureBastionSubnet and configure the target VMs' NSGs to allow inbound TCP 443 from the AzureBastionSubnet CIDR (10.0.2.0/24). This is sufficient for Bastion to connect to the VMs.
choices.explanation: While NSG rules allowing 443 from the Bastion subnet are needed on the TARGET VM NSGs, this alone is insufficient. The Bastion resource also needs the 'BastionHost' reader role on the target VMs or their resource group to initiate connections on behalf of the user.

choices.text: Azure Bastion requires the target VMs to have a Standard-tier public IP address assigned, which is used as the target for the RDP/SSH tunnel through Bastion.
choices.explanation: Azure Bastion specifically ELIMINATES the need for public IPs on target VMs. Bastion connects to VMs using their private IP addresses over the internal Azure network — no public IP on the target VM is required or used.

questions.explanation: Azure Bastion requires: (1) dedicated AzureBastionSubnet (/26 or larger), (2) NSG allowing 443 inbound + RDP/SSH outbound to target subnets, (3) BastionHost role on target VM resource group. No public IPs on target VMs.

questions.question: You have two virtual networks: VNet-A (10.0.0.0/16) and VNet-B (10.1.0.0/16) that must be connected. You configure VNet peering from VNet-A to VNet-B. The peering status shows 'Connected' but VMs in VNet-A cannot communicate with VMs in VNet-B. What is the MOST likely cause?

choices.text: The peering was configured from VNet-A to VNet-B but the reciprocal peering from VNet-B to VNet-A was not configured. Peering is unidirectional in the Azure resource model — two separate peering links must be created.
choices.explanation: Azure VNet peering is configured independently on each VNet — creating a peering from VNet-A to VNet-B does NOT automatically create the reverse link. VNet-B must also have a peering defined pointing back to VNet-A for bidirectional communication. Without the reverse peering, return traffic from VNet-B cannot reach VNet-A.

choices.text: VNet-A and VNet-B have overlapping IP address ranges (both use 10.x.x.x space), causing Azure's routing engine to drop packets between the two VNets.
choices.explanation: 10.0.0.0/16 (VNet-A: 10.0.0.0 – 10.0.255.255) and 10.1.0.0/16 (VNet-B: 10.1.0.0 – 10.1.255.255) do NOT overlap — they are distinct non-overlapping ranges. Overlapping ranges are the same subnet, e.g., 10.0.0.0/16 and 10.0.5.0/24.

choices.text: The 'Allow forwarded traffic' checkbox was left unchecked on the VNet-A to VNet-B peering, preventing traffic from being forwarded between the two VNets.
choices.explanation: 'Allow forwarded traffic' only matters if there is a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA) or VPN gateway in the VNet that is forwarding traffic FROM another VNet. Without an NVA, this setting has no effect on direct VM-to-VM communication.

choices.text: NSGs on the VM network interfaces in both VNet-A and VNet-B are blocking the traffic. VNet peering does not automatically override NSG rules.
choices.explanation: NSGs can block traffic, but if the peering status is 'Connected' and VMs can't communicate, the first and most common cause is missing reciprocal peering configuration. NSG misconfiguration is a secondary concern after verifying the peering is correctly configured on both sides.

questions.explanation: Azure VNet peering requires TWO separate peering resources — one on each VNet. The 'Connected' status on one side only means that ONE direction of the peering link is active. Without the reverse peering, return traffic cannot reach the source VNet.

questions.question: You are designing a hub-and-spoke virtual network topology. The hub VNet contains an Azure Firewall Premium with IDPS (Intrusion Detection and Prevention System) and the spoke VNets contain workloads that must inspect all outbound internet traffic through the hub firewall. Which spoke VNet configuration ensures ALL outbound traffic from spoke VMs is routed through the hub firewall?

choices.text: On each spoke VNet, configure a User Defined Route (UDR) with a 0.0.0.0/0 route pointing to the Azure Firewall private IP address in the hub VNet. Enable 'IP forwarding' on the Azure Firewall's NIC. Configure the firewall policy with DNAT rules for outbound traffic inspection.
choices.explanation: UDR with 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to Azure Firewall is correct. However, Azure Firewall Premium with IDPS handles SNAT for outbound traffic — no IP forwarding needs to be enabled on the firewall's NIC (that's for NVAs, not Azure Firewall). DNAT rules are for inbound traffic from internet to spoke, not for outbound spoke traffic.

choices.text: On each spoke VNet, create a UDR with a 0.0.0.0/0 route pointing to the Azure Firewall's internal load balancer IP in the hub VNet. In the hub VNet, configure 'GatewayTransit' on the peering to allow spokes to use the hub firewall for internet-bound traffic.
choices.explanation: Azure Firewall is not accessed via a gateway load balancer — it's deployed as a standalone zonal resource with its own direct IP. Gateway Transit is used for VPN Gateway (not Azure Firewall) in hub-spoke topologies. The correct approach uses UDRs pointing directly to the Azure Firewall's IP, not a gateway load balancer.

choices.text: Deploy an NVA-based firewall in the hub (not Azure Firewall) and configure route-based VPN gateway in the hub with BGP-enabled peering to propagate default routes to spokes via BGP.
choices.explanation: This is a valid hub-spoke design using NVAs and BGP route propagation, but the question specifically asks about Azure Firewall. Azure Firewall uses a different routing mechanism — UDRs with 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to the firewall's IP, not BGP route propagation.

choices.text: On each spoke VNet, configure a UDR with 0.0.0.0/0 route pointing to the Azure Firewall private IP address as the next hop. On the hub VNet peering settings for each spoke, enable 'Use the remote virtual network's gateway or Route Server'.
choices.explanation: 'Use remote VNet gateway' (Gateway Transit) allows spokes to use the hub's network virtual appliance (Azure Firewall) for traffic routing. This in combination with a UDR in spokes pointing 0.0.0.0/0 to the firewall's IP ensures all internet-bound traffic from spokes routes through the hub firewall.

questions.explanation: Hub-spoke traffic inspection with Azure Firewall requires: (1) UDR in spokes with 0.0.0.0/0 → Firewall IP, (2) Gateway Transit enabled on the hub-to-spoke peering. Azure Firewall does NOT require IP forwarding — it natively handles next-hop routing.

questions.question: You have an Azure Application Gateway v2 deployed in a VNet with two backend pools: Pool A (VMs in Subnet-A) and Pool B (VMs in Subnet-B). The Application Gateway has a WAF policy enabled. You need to ensure that traffic to Pool B is allowed ONLY from specific client IP addresses, while Pool A is accessible from anywhere. How should you configure this?

choices.text: On the Application Gateway's backend pool B, enable 'Pick host name from backend target' and configure a NSG on Subnet-B to allow traffic only from the specific client IP range. This provides IP-based access restriction at the network layer independent of the WAF policy.
choices.explanation: Application Gateway WAF policies operate at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) and do not provide IP-based access restriction at the pool level. IP restriction for backend pools must be implemented via NSGs on the backend subnets (Subnet-B in this case). The NSG blocks non-whitelisted IPs before traffic reaches the Application Gateway backend.

choices.text: Create a WAF policy with an IP restriction match condition targeting backend pool B, with the specific allowed IP ranges as the match variable. Apply this WAF policy to backend pool B.
choices.explanation: WAF policies in Azure Application Gateway v2 support IP restriction via 'IpRestiction' rules in the OWASP CRS configuration, but these apply to the frontend listener (incoming traffic), not to individual backend pools. WAF cannot selectively restrict traffic to specific backend pools.

choices.text: Configure the Application Gateway with two separate WAF policies: WAF-Policy-A applied to the listener for Pool A (no IP restriction), and WAF-Policy-B applied to the listener for Pool B with an IP restriction rule. Use separate listeners with different frontend ports.
choices.explanation: WAF IP restriction rules apply to incoming traffic at the frontend listener, not to which backend pool serves a request. A listener can route to different backend pools based on URL path or hostname, but the IP restriction is evaluated before routing — it would block all traffic from non-whitelisted IPs to that listener regardless of which pool they would be routed to.

choices.text: Deploy two separate Application Gateway instances: one for Pool A (no IP filter) and one for Pool B (with IP filter NSG on its backend subnet). This is the only way to achieve pool-level IP isolation.
choices.explanation: While this technically works, it is not necessary — NSG-level IP restriction on the backend subnet is sufficient without requiring a second Application Gateway instance, which would add cost and complexity.

questions.explanation: WAF policies operate at the frontend listener (L7), not at the backend pool level. IP-based restriction for specific backend pools must be implemented via NSGs on the backend subnets — this is the correct layered security approach.

questions.question: You are troubleshooting a site-to-site VPN connection between an on-premises network and an Azure VNet. The on-premises VPN device is configured with the correct public IP, shared key, and VPN gateway IP address. The Azure VPN gateway shows 'Connected' but no traffic is flowing. What should you check FIRST?

choices.text: Check the effective routes on the Azure VPN gateway's virtual network gateway using 'az network vnet show-gateway-route' to verify that Azure has learned the on-premises prefixes via BGP (if BGP is enabled) or that the local network gateway prefixes are correctly configured.
choices.explanation: 'Connected' status on the VPN gateway only means the IPSec tunnel is established — it does not guarantee that routing is configured correctly. If the local network gateway's address prefix doesn't match the on-premises network, or BGP peering is down, traffic will not flow. The first diagnostic step is checking learned/installed routes on the gateway.

choices.text: Verify that the on-premises VPN device's 'Phase 2 (IPSec)' encryption and authentication algorithms match those configured on the Azure VPN gateway policy. Mismatched Phase 2 settings cause the tunnel to connect but not pass traffic.
choices.explanation: If Phase 2 (IPSec SA) settings mismatched, the IPSec tunnel would NOT connect at all — Azure VPN gateway 'Connected' status indicates the IKE Phase 1 and Phase 2 negotiations completed successfully. Phase mismatch = no connection, not connected-but-no-traffic.

choices.text: Check the Azure VPN gateway SKU to ensure it supports the throughput requirements. The Basic SKU has a 100 Mbps aggregate throughput limit which may cause apparent 'no traffic' behavior when exceeded.
choices.explanation: Throughput limits on Basic SKU would manifest as slow traffic, not zero traffic. A throughput limit causes degraded performance, not a complete absence of traffic flow.

choices.text: Verify that the 'Enable BGP' setting was unchecked on the Azure VPN gateway if BGP is not configured on the on-premises device. Having BGP enabled on one side only causes traffic to fail silently.
choices.explanation: BGP enabled on only one side causes BGP peering to fail, but the IPSec tunnel would still operate using static routing. If BGP is not configured on the on-premises device, the Azure VPN gateway should have BGP disabled as well. However, BGP mismatch causes routing failure, not tunnel connection failure.

questions.explanation: VPN gateway 'Connected' = tunnel established. 'No traffic flowing' = routing problem (most likely). Check the effective routes and local network gateway prefix configuration first — the on-premises network prefix must be correctly configured in the local network gateway.

questions.question: You have a VNet with an Azure VPN Gateway (route-based) and an ExpressRoute gateway deployed in the same gateway subnet. The on-premises network is connected via both VPN and ExpressRoute. Users report that VPN-connected users can access Azure VMs but ExpressRoute-connected users cannot. What is the MOST likely cause?

choices.text: The ExpressRoute gateway and VPN Gateway are co-located in the same gateway subnet, which causes asymmetric routing: ExpressRoute traffic returns via VPN (which has a more specific route) instead of via ExpressRoute. This causes return traffic to be dropped by the on-premises router.
choices.explanation: Co-located VPN and ExpressRoute gateways in the same subnet can cause asymmetric routing — when both paths exist, the return traffic may prefer the VPN route over ExpressRoute (especially if VPN has a more specific 0.0.0.0/0 or local network route). The on-premises router drops return traffic that doesn't match the incoming ExpressRoute interface. Solution: use 'Force Tunneling' via VPN or configure routing preferences to prevent asymmetric routing.

choices.text: The ExpressRoute circuit's primary and secondary peering must both be configured with the same route filters before traffic can flow. VPN traffic works because it uses the secondary peering by default.
choices.explanation: ExpressRoute peering configuration affects which routes are advertised/received, not whether the circuit connects. Both peering sessions can be active simultaneously. VPN and ExpressRoute are independent connection types — the peering status doesn't affect VPN connectivity.

choices.text: The ExpressRoute gateway SKU (Standard) does not support co-location with VPN Gateway. The VPN Gateway must be deleted before ExpressRoute can route traffic.
choices.explanation: Co-location of ExpressRoute and VPN Gateway in the same gateway subnet is supported — both can be deployed together. However, the Standard SKU of ExpressRoute Gateway supports up to 10 Gbps but when co-located with VPN Gateway, the combined throughput is shared. This is not the cause of routing failure.

choices.text: The NSG on the gateway subnet is blocking UDP 500 and 4500 (IPSec) for VPN while allowing GRE for ExpressRoute, causing VPN to work and ExpressRoute to fail.
choices.explanation: ExpressRoute uses MPLS or Layer 2 connectivity via the Microsoft Enterprise Edge (MSEE) router — it doesn't use GRE or IPSec at the Azure gateway subnet level. The NSG on the gateway subnet should allow traffic for both but blocking VPN ports wouldn't affect ExpressRoute.

questions.explanation: Co-located VPN + ExpressRoute gateways in the same subnet commonly cause asymmetric routing — both paths exist to on-premises, and the return path may prefer the VPN route. Use 'Use remote gateway' on the ExpressRoute connection or configure route preference to resolve asymmetric routing.

questions.question: You are designing a solution that requires a private DNS zone for an internal application. The application VMs are in a VNet named 'VNet-Prod' (10.0.0.0/16). You need to ensure that VMs in VNet-Prod can resolve the internal DNS name 'app.internal.company.com' to a private IP address. Which two configurations are required to achieve this?

choices.text: Create an Azure Private DNS zone named 'internal.company.com'. Link the zone to VNet-Prod using a virtual network link with auto-registration enabled.
choices.explanation: Azure Private DNS zones provide DNS resolution for workloads without needing a custom DNS server. Linking the zone to VNet-Prod and enabling auto-registration automatically creates DNS A records for VMs in that VNet as they are provisioned and destroyed.

choices.text: Configure the VMs' DNS servers in VNet-Prod to use the Azure-provided DNS (168.63.129.16), which is the default for VNets without a custom DNS server.
choices.explanation: Azure-provided DNS (168.63.129.16) is the default for basic DNS within a VNet, but for Private DNS zones to work, the VNet's custom DNS servers must include 168.63.129.16 OR use Azure DNS (4.2.2.1/2.2.2.2). Without this, VMs cannot resolve the Private DNS zone name.

choices.text: Deploy an Azure Virtual Network NAT Gateway on VNet-Prod's subnet to provide outbound internet connectivity for VMs, which also enables DNS resolution for private DNS zones.
choices.explanation: NAT Gateway provides SNAT for outbound internet access and does not affect DNS resolution. DNS resolution for Private DNS zones is handled by Azure DNS or custom DNS servers, not by NAT Gateway.

choices.text: Register the domain 'company.com' with Azure DNS and create a CNAME record 'app.internal.company.com' pointing to the internal load balancer's private IP address in the Azure Public DNS zone.
choices.explanation: Azure Public DNS resolves public-facing names, not internal private IP addresses. 'internal.company.com' must be in a Private DNS zone (not Public). Public DNS cannot resolve private IP addresses — it only resolves public IPs. Auto-registration in a linked Private DNS zone is the correct mechanism.

questions.explanation: Azure Private DNS zone with a virtual network link (auto-registration) + VNet DNS server configuration (Azure-provided DNS 168.63.129.16 or Azure DNS 4.2.2.1) are both required for VMs to resolve private DNS names.

questions.question: You have an Azure VNet with a subnet that contains an Azure Standard Load Balancer (internal) frontend IP configuration, multiple backend pool VMs, and a health probe. The health probe is configured on TCP port 3389 (RDP). One of the backend VMs becomes unresponsive but its OS is still running. The load balancer stops sending traffic to this VM. What is the MOST likely reason and what should you change?

choices.text: The health probe is probing RDP port 3389 which will fail as soon as the application becomes unresponsive even if the OS is running — the probe should target an application-specific port that reflects actual application health. Change the health probe to TCP port 443 (HTTPS) or a custom application port.
choices.explanation: Probing port 3389 works fine when the VM is responsive. The issue states the OS is still running but the application is unresponsive — a port 3389 probe would still succeed because the RDP service is responding. The problem must be elsewhere.

choices.text: The health probe uses TCP port 3389 which may be blocked by an NSG rule on the VM preventing the load balancer from probing the port, even though the VM is healthy. The LB removes the VM because it interprets blocked probes as VM failure.
choices.explanation: Azure Standard Load Balancer health probes MUST be allowed through NSGs on the backend VMs. If NSG blocks the probe port, the load balancer sees the probe as failed and removes the VM from the backend pool — even if the VM is perfectly healthy. This is one of the most common Standard LB misconfigurations.

choices.text: The load balancer's health probe timeout is too short (default 31 seconds) — the VM's slow RDP response is causing probe timeouts. Increase the probe interval from 5 seconds to 30 seconds.
choices.explanation: Probe timeout being too short would cause intermittent probe failures, not consistent failure. The timeout should be at least 3x the interval (default: 5s interval × 31s timeout). Increasing the timeout from 31 to 30s would actually reduce the threshold, making failures MORE likely.

choices.text: The backend VM was removed from the load balancer's backend pool because it exceeded the 'Number of probes' threshold (default 3) for the TCP 3389 probe. Re-add the VM to the backend pool and configure a higher probe threshold.
choices.explanation: Standard Load Balancer doesn't have a concept of 'removing a VM from the backend pool because it exceeded probe failures' in the way described — it simply stops routing NEW connections to unhealthy instances. The VM remains in the backend pool but is marked as unhealthy. There's no need to 're-add' it — it becomes healthy again once probes succeed.

questions.explanation: Azure Standard LB health probes must be explicitly allowed in NSGs on backend VMs. If NSG blocks the probe, the VM is marked unhealthy regardless of its actual health — this is the #1 cause of Standard LB probe failures.

questions.question: You need to configure Azure DNS private resolver to enable on-premises DNS servers to resolve Azure private DNS records without deploying Azure VMs as DNS servers. The on-premises network uses Windows DNS servers. Which Azure DNS configuration achieves this?

choices.text: Deploy an Azure DNS Private Resolver with an inbound endpoint in the VNet and an outbound endpoint with a conditional forwarder rule that forwards DNS queries for 'internal.company.com' to the Azure-provided DNS server (168.63.129.16) IP address. Configure the on-premises DNS server to forward queries for 'internal.company.com' to the inbound endpoint's IP address.
choices.explanation: Azure DNS Private Resolver allows on-premises DNS servers to query Azure Private DNS zones without needing a DNS server VM in Azure. The inbound endpoint provides a forwarder IP in the VNet for on-premises DNS to send queries to. The conditional forwarder on the outbound endpoint routes 'internal.company.com' queries to Azure DNS.

choices.text: Deploy Azure DNS Private Resolver with only an outbound endpoint. Configure the on-premises DNS servers to use the outbound endpoint's IP as a forwarder for the 'internal.company.com' domain.
choices.explanation: Outbound endpoints are used for DNS queries FROM Azure TO on-premises, not FROM on-premises TO Azure. On-premises DNS servers need an inbound endpoint IP to send queries into Azure — without an inbound endpoint, there is no IP address in the VNet for on-premises DNS to forward to.

choices.text: Deploy an Azure DNS Private Resolver in the VNet and configure a private link service for the Azure DNS service, allowing on-premises DNS to resolve private DNS records over the ExpressRoute private peering.
choices.explanation: Azure DNS Private Resolver is the correct service for this scenario. Private Link provides private access to Azure PaaS services, not DNS resolution. DNS resolution for private zones requires Private Resolver, not Private Link.

choices.text: Use Azure Private Link to expose Azure DNS as a private endpoint in the VNet, and configure on-premises DNS servers to use the private endpoint IP as their primary DNS server for all queries.
choices.explanation: Azure DNS Private Link is for accessing Azure DNS service endpoints over a private connection, not for resolving Private DNS zones. Private Link cannot be used with Azure DNS for private zone resolution — Azure DNS Private Resolver is the designated service for hybrid DNS resolution.

questions.explanation: Azure DNS Private Resolver's inbound endpoint provides a VNet-located IP for on-premises DNS servers to forward Azure-private DNS queries to. Combined with conditional forwarding rules, it enables hybrid DNS resolution without VM-based DNS servers.

questions.question: You have an Azure VNet with an Azure NAT Gateway attached to the subnet. The NAT Gateway is configured with a public IP address. You need to ensure that inbound RDP connections from the internet to VMs in this subnet are blocked, while outbound internet access from VMs is still possible via the NAT Gateway. Which configuration achieves this?

choices.text: NAT Gateway does not support inbound internet traffic — it only handles outbound SNAT. Inbound RDP must be blocked at the NSG level (deny TCP 3389 from internet) on the subnet NSG. Outbound traffic from VMs uses the NAT Gateway automatically for outbound connections.
choices.explanation: Azure NAT Gateway is strictly an outbound SNAT solution — it does not accept inbound connections from the internet. All inbound internet traffic to VMs in a NAT Gateway-attached subnet must be blocked at the NSG level. Outbound traffic is automatically routed through the NAT Gateway without any NSG rules. This is the correct layered configuration: NSG blocks inbound, NAT Gateway handles outbound.

choices.text: Configure the NAT Gateway to only allow outbound traffic by setting 'Inbound' to 'Deny'. This automatically creates an NSG rule that blocks all inbound RDP traffic.
choices.explanation: NAT Gateway does not have an 'Inbound' setting that controls access. NAT Gateway has no inbound configuration — it is purely an outbound SNAT mechanism. The inbound blocking must be done via NSG, not via NAT Gateway configuration.

choices.text: Remove the public IP from the NAT Gateway and assign a new public IP with an NSG rule that allows only outbound TCP 443 and blocks all other outbound traffic. This ensures only HTTPS outbound is allowed and RDP inbound is implicitly blocked.
choices.explanation: Removing the public IP from NAT Gateway would break its ability to perform outbound SNAT — VMs would lose internet access entirely. The NSG suggestion also mischaracterizes the problem — the issue is INBOUND RDP blocking, not outbound HTTPS allowance.

choices.text: Deploy an Azure Firewall with a network rule that denies TCP 3389 from the internet and a NAT rule that allows outbound RDP to specific management IPs. Attach the Firewall to the subnet instead of the NAT Gateway.
choices.explanation: Azure Firewall is overkill for blocking inbound RDP and is not required — NSG rules are sufficient. Replacing NAT Gateway with Azure Firewall adds significant cost and complexity for a simple RDP block.

questions.explanation: Azure NAT Gateway is outbound-only SNAT. Inbound RDP blocking must be done via subnet-level or NIC-level NSG. Outbound internet access from VMs is automatically handled by NAT Gateway without any additional NSG configuration.

questions.question: You have an Azure VNet with a subnet named 'Subnet-App' (10.0.1.0/24) that contains an App Service with a regional VNet integration enabled. The App Service needs to connect to an Azure SQL Database named 'SQL-DB1' in a different resource group, using its private endpoint 'sql-dbsspriv001.private.database.windows.net'. The App Service fails to connect to the database. What is the MOST likely cause?

choices.text: The private endpoint for SQL-DB1 is in a different VNet than the App Service's integration VNet (Subnet-App is not connected to the VNet containing the private endpoint). App Service regional VNet integration only routes traffic for addresses within the integrated VNet — it cannot reach private endpoints in other VNets.
choices.explanation: App Service regional VNet integration routes traffic only for the integrated VNet's address space. If the SQL private endpoint is in a DIFFERENT VNet (even in the same region), the App Service cannot reach it without VNet peering connecting the two VNets. This is a common misconfiguration when SQL private endpoints are deployed in a dedicated 'data' VNet separate from the App Service VNet.

choices.text: The App Service does not support TLS connections to Azure SQL Database private endpoints — it requires a public endpoint connection with firewall rules configured.
choices.explanation: App Service fully supports TLS connections to Azure SQL Database via private endpoints. App Service outbound traffic to private endpoints is supported when VNet integration is properly configured with the private endpoint's VNet.

choices.text: Azure SQL Database private endpoints require the 'Allow trusted Microsoft services' firewall setting to be enabled on the SQL Server, which is blocking the App Service connection.
choices.explanation: 'Allow trusted Microsoft services' applies to the SQL Server's public IP firewall rules, not to private endpoints.
